inbound.subject: Starting over after a layoff
inbound.body: Hi coach, I want to get back to running after a messy few months. My big goal is to get healthy enough to race a half marathon again this spring. Right now I mostly need help rebuilding without doing something stupid.

outbound.subject: Re: Starting over after a layoff
outbound.text:
Nice to meet you — glad you reached out. I hear you: you want to rebuild running safely and get back to racing a half marathon this spring without repeating whatever went wrong over the last few months.
Before I write a safe, specific return-to-running plan, I need a few key details about your recent history and current availability. Once you answer the short questions below I’ll send a conservative 3–4 week return plan (with the why behind each session) and an outline of how we’ll progress from there.
Brief questions (short answers are fine):
1) What exactly happened in the “messy months”? (injury, illness, burnout, busy life — short timeline)
2) Any current pain or symptoms? Where, what activities make it worse, and how would you rate it 0–10?
3) Have you seen a clinician for this, and are there any restrictions or diagnostics I should know about?
4) When did you last run and what did that run feel like?
5) Usual peak weekly mileage before the layoff, and how much are you running now (if any)?
6) How many days/week and time per day can you realistically commit right now?
7) Do you have a target race date (or a rough “spring” month) and a performance priority (finish, pace goal, just healthy rebuild)?
8) Any other activities (cycling, swimming, strength work) or previous injuries I should know about?
9) What shoes and surfaces do you usually run on?
10) Can you upload recent GPS files (Strava/Runkeeper/GPX), a short training log, or a photo of any problem area if relevant?
Optional — a very conservative session you can do now if you want to get moving (skip this if you’re unsure or in pain):
- 5 min easy walk warm-up, then 3–5 rounds of 1 minute easy jog + 4 minutes walk, finish with 5 min walk cooldown (total ~25–35 minutes). Keep effort conversational; if anything hurts, stop and rest or choose a brisk walk or bike instead.
Next steps: after your answers I’ll send a 3–4 week conservative return plan with the purpose of each session, the markers I’ll watch (pain during/after sessions, next-day soreness, ability to complete sessions, sleep/fatigue), and how we’ll check in and progress. Plan on a weekly check-in so we can adjust—message sooner if pain spikes or something doesn’t feel right.
Send any GPS files or photos with your reply and I’ll get the return plan to you. If you want, try the optional run/walk and tell me how it feels when you reply.
Coach Sarah

inbound.subject: A little more context
inbound.body: A bit more context: I used to run consistently before the layoff and I'd call myself intermediate. I can train four days a week most weeks if we keep it realistic. The thing that nags me is mild Achilles tightness when I get greedy. I also do better when the plan feels structured.

outbound.subject: Re: A little more context
outbound.text:
Thanks — this extra detail helps a lot. I hear you: intermediate runner, used to consistent miles, can do four days/week if it stays realistic, Achilles gets tight when you 'get greedy', and you perform better with structure. Good call sharing this up front.
Quick reflection: goal is to rebuild consistency while protecting the Achilles. You can train four days/week, you want structure, and you’re sensitive to load increases — so I’ve built Week 1 deliberately conservative and repeatable.
Week 1 (skeleton + session details)
- Day 1 — Easy aerobic: 20–30 minutes easy run. Warm up 5 min walk/easy jog, then easy running (conversational pace), finish with 3–5 min easy jog/walk. Cues: light cadence, posture tall, short stride.
- Day 2 — Strength (lower-leg focus): ~25–30 minutes total. See the strength outline below. Start with bodyweight only; slow controlled reps.
- Day 3 — Recovery/mobility day: 15–20 minutes gentle mobility (ankle circles, calf/soleus stretches held gently, hip/glute activation) + easy movement (walk or light bike 10–15 min).
- Day 4 — Easy aerobic: 20–30 minutes easy run, same cues as Day 1.
Why Week 1 is like this
- Priority: complete sessions without symptom worsening. Early work is about consistency and tissue confidence, not fitness gains. Easy runs keep blood flow without overloading the Achilles; the strength day starts to build calf control and tolerance. Avoiding intensity and mileage jumps lowers re-injury risk.
Strength session (specific, simple)
- Single-leg calf raise (slow): 3 sets x 6–8 reps per leg. Tempo ~2s up, 3s down. Use support for balance.
- Seated calf raises or double-leg slow raise: 2 sets x 12–15 reps (focus on full range and controlled lowering).
- Banded ankle control (dorsiflexion/eversion as tolerated): 3 sets x 10 reps each direction.
Loading notes: start bodyweight. Only add small external load (light dumbbell or backpack) after two pain-free weeks of this work. No speed work or hops this week.
Monitoring / what to report after each session
Please send a short note after each session with:
- Pain (0–10) during the session, right after, and the next morning
- Any new swelling (yes/no)
- Stiffness (0–10) and whether it’s improving or worse by next morning
- Sleep (hours) and general energy/mood (0–10)
A one-line format works best (e.g., “Day1 run: during 1, after 1, AM 1; no swelling; stiffness 1; slept 7; energy 7”).
If / then safety rules
- If pain increases by ~2 points from your baseline during or after a session, stop that activity immediately and rest.
- If you get new swelling or pain >4/10, stop running and switch to non-impact cross-training (bike or pool). Tell me and I’ll pull back the plan.
- If the week destabilizes overall, we’ll reduce to only the easiest anchor sessions (walking/bike + the mobility work) until symptoms settle.
- Note: no hard sessions at any time while risk is in the red tier.
Coaching cadence and expectations
- I need a short report after each session this week (use the one-line format above). I’ll check those midweek and again after Week 1 and will respond within 24 hours with any adjustments.
- Progression rule: we’ll repeat this conservative week until you complete it without symptom worsening across sessions. Only after a stable week will we increase load or add intensity.
If you want rehab detail, I can send a short guide/video on the Alfredson eccentric heel-drop protocol (practical demo) — only if you want to include that as part of your home work.
Questions? Quick replies are perfect. This plan is flexible — we’ll adjust based on your reports and keep the priority on control and consistency.

inbound.subject: Profile details and target race
inbound.body: Here is the missing context more directly. Primary goal: run a half marathon on 2026-05-17 and finish feeling strong. Time availability: 4 sessions per week and about 4 to 5 hours total. Experience level: intermediate. Constraints: mild Achilles tightness, busy work mornings on Tuesdays, and I prefer structured guidance.

outbound.subject: Re: Profile details and target race
outbound.text:
Thanks — this is exactly the missing detail I needed. I’ve built the updated week around your half-marathon target (2026-05-17), your 4-sessions/week availability, and the Achilles caution — and avoided packing anything into Tuesday mornings since those are busy for you.
Confirming: half marathon 2026-05-17, 4 sessions/week, mild Achilles tightness, Tuesday mornings blocked, you prefer structured guidance. We’re two weeks into the return-to-training block.
Coaching posture (short): conservative return-to-training. Priority = consistent, repeatable sessions + symptom control. No intensity this week and do not make up missed sessions later in the week.
This week — session-by-session (fits your 4–5 hour weekly limit):
- Session A (easy aerobic): 25–35 min easy run — conversational pace, focus on cadence and tall posture.
- Session B (easy aerobic): 25–35 min easy run OR 30–40 min easy walk/run if you’re tired. Avoid Tuesday morning; schedule this later in the day or pick a different day.
- Session C (reduced-intensity / low-cost): 20–30 min easy movement — bike, elliptical, brisk walk, or an easy 20-min jog only if you feel good. Treat this as low-cost volume.
- Session D (strength): 25–30 min lower-leg + glute/hip routine (bodyweight now; add load later). Include calf raises (double → single), single-leg balance, glute bridges, and hip-hinge patterning.
Practical cues: keep cadence light, posture tall, controlled landing. Avoid hops, bounds, strides, or tempos until we reintroduce specificity.
Pain-monitoring rules (exact): stop the session if any of the following occur — pain increases by roughly 2/10 versus your baseline, visible swelling appears, or morning pain >4/10. If that happens, downgrade to an easy walk or mobility-only session and message me a one-line report.
Session report (one-line) after every session: pain during / pain after / AM pain, swelling yes/no, stiffness level, sleep (hrs) / energy. Example: “Run done — during 1/10, after 2/10, AM 1/10; swelling no; stiffness mild; sleep 7h; energy ok.”
Progression rule: we need 7–10 consistently symptom-free training days before adding any targeted intensity. When we progress we’ll change only one thing at a time: first lock repeatable consistency → then a small volume step → then low-grade specificity.
Quick resource: for a short how-to on calf loading, search “Alfredson heel drop exercises video” or watch a physio-led 5–10 minute demo (useful background—follow your clinician’s advice if you’re working with one).
Logistics: tell me which days you prefer for the 4 sessions (remember Tuesday morning is out) and whether you want the 25–30 min strength session on a run-adjacent day or as a standalone day. I’ll lock the week once you confirm.
Coach

inbound.subject: Availability update for this week
inbound.body: Availability update for this week. Event date: 2026-05-17. Days available: 4. Pain score: 2 out of 10. Risk candidate feels green. I can run Monday, Wednesday, Friday, and Sunday. Week is not chaotic. Schedule variability is medium because work may move one session by a few hours.

outbound.subject: Re: Availability update for this week
outbound.text:
Thanks for the clear availability and the pain update. Four sessions fit nicely with your target race and your return-to-running plan, and I’ll keep the load conservative to protect the Achilles as you rebuild.
Here’s Week 1 plan tailored to your four-session week and current symptoms. The aim is consistent, low-risk loading that rebuilds mileage without aggravating pain.
We’re three weeks into rebuilding consistency in Week 3 of your return-to-running plan, 18 weeks out from the May 17, 2026 event.
Target race date: May 17, 2026. Four sessions/week. Pain level: 2/10. Risk status: green.
Week 1 structure (Mon, Wed, Fri, Sun):
- Monday: Easy aerobic run (roughly 20–25 minutes, at a comfortable pace).
- Wednesday: Lower-leg strength session (25–30 minutes) focusing on tendon-friendly movements (calf raises, ankle mobility, hip stability).
- Friday: Low-cost movement session (20–30 minutes) including mobility, sequencing work, and light core to support running mechanics.
- Sunday: Easy aerobic run (25–35 minutes, progressive but still easy pace).
Note: four sessions total, no high-load or tempo work this week; the two easy runs establish base mileage while the strength and movement days support injury prevention.
Rationale/why this structure for you:
You’ve had a recent layoff and a mild Achilles sensitivity. A four-session base week keeps volume manageable, distributes load across key days, and prioritizes durability over intensity. The plan uses two easy runs to rebuild cadence and endurance, one dedicated strength day to fortify tendon and supporting muscles, and one mobility-focused day to sustain joints and movement quality. This is aligned with a conservative return-to-training strategy and your event date, reducing re-injury risk while laying a solid foundation for future progression.
Pain monitoring and adjustments:
If pain or fatigue rises meaningfully (e.g., pain > 3/10, swelling, morning stiffness), downgrade the session to easy and avoid adding load later in the week. Do not attempt to “make up” missed intensity later in the week.
Cadence and reporting:
Please send a one-line session report after each workout (current pain level, any swelling, stiffness, energy, and a quick note on how the session felt). This helps us stay safely on track and adjust quickly.
Next steps and confidence:
Please confirm which day you’d prefer for the strength day (Wed or Sun) given your schedule, and whether you’d like Fri’s movement day to be more mobility-focused or include light stability work. If anything in the plan doesn’t feel feasible this week, we can adapt immediately.

inbound.subject: First check-in after an easy run
inbound.body: Training check-in. Event date: 2026-05-17. Days available: 4. Pain score: 2 out of 10. Risk candidate is green. I did a run for 45m and 6 km yesterday. Felt good, energy ok, little sore, slept well. Missed sessions count: 0. I want to keep building carefully.

outbound.subject: Re: First check-in after an easy run
outbound.text:
Great to hear your check-in and that the last easy run felt solid with only light soreness. With an event date of 2026-05-17 and four training days available, we’ll keep building carefully and guard the Achilles.
We’re three weeks into rebuilding consistency in this return-to-training block. There are 17 weeks until race day, and we’re keeping the week structure four days and recovery-friendly to support base-building.
Here’s Week 1 plan (four sessions):
- Mon — easy aerobic 20–25 minutes
- Wed — lower-leg strength session (tendon-friendly, 25–30 minutes)
- Fri — low-cost movement day (mobility/core, 20–30 minutes)
- Sun — easy aerobic 25–35 minutes
Rationale: tempo work is not included this week to prioritize safe base-building and protect the Achilles as you rebuild aerobic capacity and tendon resilience.
Pain and load guardrails:
- If pain or fatigue worsens (or morning pain rises above 3/10), downgrade that session to easy and do not try to catch up later in the week.
- We’ll monitor daily and keep loads gentle; there’s no requirement to “make up” intensity later in the week.
A few quick alignments:
- Please share a one-line post-session report after each workout.
- Let me know your preference for the strength day (Wed vs Sun) so we can lock it in as a standing pattern.
- Cadence and posture: keep cadence light and posture tall as you move.
If you’re good with this, I’ll treat Week 1 as the baseline and adjust in real time based on how you feel each day. Please also confirm whether you want the strength day to be run-adjacent to a run or as a standalone session.

inbound.subject: Long run felt fine
inbound.body: Long run update: I got through 75 minutes this morning and honestly I feel ok after the long run. The Achilles is there in the background but not angry, and I slept well last night. I'd like to keep the same four-day rhythm this week if that still makes sense.

outbound.subject: Re: Long run felt fine
outbound.text:
Great to hear the 75-minute long run felt manageable and the Achilles issue is staying quiet so far. I'm with you on keeping the four-day rhythm this week—steady, controlled, and safe.
We're four weeks into this return-to-training block, with 16 weeks to race day.
Here's Week 1 skeleton, tailored to your base-building and Achilles background, plus the rationale for this cadence:
- Mon: easy_aerobic 20-25 min at a relaxed pace
- Wed: lower-leg tendon-friendly strength 25-30 min (tendon/ankle-focused, controlled loading)
- Fri: mobility/core 20-30 min (hip/glute emphasis, ankle mobility, core stability)
- Sun: easy_aerobic 25-35 min, easy pace
Key cues: cadence light, posture tall, avoid any intensity or hills. Prioritize sleep and simple recovery habits to support adaptation.
Pain monitoring framework: if pain or morning stiffness rises beyond a mild baseline, downgrade the session to easy and do not attempt to make up missed load later in the week. Maintain the four-day rhythm and listen to your body.
Post-session check-in: please send a one-line report after each workout describing pain during/after/morning, swelling, stiffness, energy, and sleep. Also confirm your preferred strength day (Wed or Sun) and whether you want the strength work run-adjacent or standalone.
Safety signals and adjustments: if pain or fatigue rises meaningfully, downgrade the session to easy. Do not ramp up intensity or volume to chase a missed day later in the week.
Next steps: please reply with
- 1) your preferred strength day (Wed or Sun) and whether you want it run-adjacent or standalone
- 2) your plan for post-session check-ins (or any adjustments)
- 3) any current barriers to sleep or recovery, so we can address them quickly.
We're aligned to steady progress this week while protecting the Achilles and rebuilding consistency toward your May event.

inbound.subject: How easy should easy feel?
inbound.body: Quick question: on these comeback runs, how easy should easy actually feel? I'm trying not to chase pace, but I also don't want to jog so slowly that the mechanics get weird.

outbound.subject: Re: How easy should easy feel?
outbound.text:
Nice question—your instinct to keep easy days truly easy is exactly what we want as you rebuild after the layoff. With your Achilles in the background, easy should stay calm and controlled rather than effortful.
Easy days should feel truly easy: you can speak in full sentences without gasping, your breathing is relaxed, and you’re not chasing pace. Use the talk test as your guide: if you can chat comfortably, you’re likely in the right zone.
For this comeback week, keep the plan conservative and controlled: no intensity, just steady, aerobic volume. Your four-day rhythm stays intact (Mon easy, Wed strength, Fri mobility/core, Sun easy). If you wake sore or notice morning stiffness rising, dial back the duration by 5–10 minutes or switch to walk-run until symptoms settle. Focus is on rebuilding comfort and consistency while protecting the Achilles. I’ll keep the cadence simple and symptom-guided as you re-load volume.
You’re five weeks into this return-to-training block, with 16 weeks to your event on 2026-05-17.
Pain/symptom monitoring and downgrade rules:
- If soreness increases or morning stiffness rises, shorten the duration by 5–10 minutes or switch to a walk-run to settle back to baseline.
- If pain limits your ability to converse or you have local Achilles tenderness that worsens with movement, pause and reassess before continuing.
Next steps — choose how you want to handle strength around the run:
- Option A (standalone): Keep Wednesday strength as a separate session on its scheduled day.
- Option B (run-adjacent): Move a light strength block to a run-adjacent day (before or after an easy run) if you prefer to group the sessions.
Please reply with your chosen option and a brief post-workout note after today’s session (how the talk test felt, any new soreness, and whether the easy pace felt truly easy).

inbound.subject: Work got messy
inbound.body: Can we adjust the week? Work is crazy and I probably only have Wednesday, Friday, and Sunday for training. I missed one session already and my stress is definitely up.

outbound.subject: Re: Work got messy
outbound.text:
Work sounds messy and you’ve already missed a session — that’s fine. I’ll simplify this week to a deliberate, minimum-effective plan and protect the Achilles while keeping progress on the long run.
I hear you: only Wed/Fri/Sun available, stress is up, and there’s background Achilles soreness — understood. We’re six weeks into the return-to-training block and 15 weeks from your event, so this conservative approach is the right call.
This week (Wed / Fri / Sun):
- Wed: 20–30 min easy run + strength, or 15–20 min standalone strength if you prefer. Keep strength low-impact and Achilles-friendly (bodyweight, hip/glute focus; no jumping).
- Fri: 25–40 min easy aerobic run (talk test — conversational pace).
- Sun: 35–60 min easy, conservative long run. If energy or Achilles aren’t good, shorten to 30–40 min, use a walk–run, or switch to easy bike/elliptical.
Conservative intensity rules:
- Every session conversational — maintain the talk test.
- No strides, no tempo efforts, no hills at race effort this week.
- If energy drops, cut time rather than push intensity.
Achilles downgrades (exact triggers and actions):
- If morning stiffness or localized tenderness increases: shorten the session by 5–10 min, or switch to walk–run or easy bike/elliptical.
- If you notice new or worsening swelling, skip the run and choose cross-train.
- If sharp pain occurs during a session, stop and swap to bike/elliptical or walk–run, and skip strength until it settles.
- If strength causes increased morning Achilles stiffness or localized tenderness, skip that strength session.
Two quick confirmations for me:
1) Which day do you want the strength session — Wed or Sun? 
2) Do you prefer run-adjacent strength (after the run) or a standalone strength session?
After each session, send one short line so I can adjust next week. Include: session, pain during/after/AM, swelling (yes/no), stiffness, sleep, energy. Example: “Wed: 25min run — no pain; AM stiffness 1/10; slept 7h; energy low.”
I’ll re-check next week and keep the plan conservative until you’re completing sessions consistently and pain-free.
